summary: This PR adds support for setting an explicit multipart boundary via a caller-supplied `Content-Type` header. The core logic is a new `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` helper in `_multipart.py` and wiring in `_models.py` and `_content.py`. While the feature works in the happy path, there are significant security concerns around insufficient validation of the user-supplied boundary value (CRLF injection, RFC 2046 character-set violations), a correctness bug where trailing whitespace is not stripped from the parsed boundary, and several test-coverage gaps. (1 finding(s) below the 0.4 confidence threshold hidden.)

findings.title: User-controlled boundary accepted without RFC 2046 validation — CRLF/header-injection risk
findings.detail:
The `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` function extracts a boundary value directly from a caller-supplied `Content-Type` header and returns it verbatim (after only stripping double-quote characters). No validation is performed against the RFC 2046 §5.1.1 allowed character set (letters, digits, and a small set of punctuation).

This creates at least two concrete security risks:
1. **CRLF/header injection**: A boundary containing `\r\n` will be embedded literally into multipart delimiter lines and potentially into `Content-Type` headers emitted by `get_headers()`, allowing an attacker who can influence the header value to inject arbitrary headers.
2. **Boundary confusion / body injection**: Characters such as `--` inside the boundary can break multipart framing.

Because the library previously always generated a safe random boundary, accepting an externally-supplied one without strict validation widens the attack surface considerably. The extracted boundary should be validated against the RFC 2046 allowed set and the function should raise a clear `ValueError` (or fall back to a random boundary) when the value is invalid. This concern is compounded by the fact that `_models.py` lines 336-347 now lets user-supplied headers bypass the previously-safe library-generated boundary entirely.

Note: the lack of trailing whitespace stripping (f0) is a separate correctness issue — see the finding below.

findings.title: Trailing whitespace not stripped from extracted boundary value
findings.detail:
The boundary extraction expression `section.strip()[len(b'boundary='):].strip(b'"')` removes double-quote characters but does **not** strip trailing (or leading) whitespace from the final boundary token.

For example, for a value like `boundary="abc" ` (space after closing quote), `.strip(b'"')` leaves a trailing space. Similarly, for `boundary= abc ` (spaces around an unquoted value), the leading/trailing spaces remain after slicing off `boundary=`. The fix is to add a final `.strip()` call: `section.strip()[len(b'boundary='):].strip(b'"').strip()`. Without this, the boundary embedded in the multipart body may not match what the library writes as the delimiter, corrupting the request.

findings.title: Using `self.headers.encoding` to encode the Content-Type value may produce incorrect bytes
findings.detail:
The code calls `self.headers.get('content-type').encode(self.headers.encoding)` to obtain the raw bytes for the content-type header. `self.headers.encoding` reflects the encoding inferred from *all* headers (defaulting to ASCII but potentially falling back to latin-1 or others if non-ASCII bytes are present in any other header). The `Content-Type` header value is always ASCII, but using a dynamically-inferred, potentially non-ASCII encoding to re-encode it is fragile.

A safer approach is to use a fixed encoding such as `.encode('latin-1')` (the correct encoding for HTTP/1.1 header field values per RFC 7230) or `.encode('ascii')`. This avoids any encoding mismatch and makes the intent explicit.

findings.title: Multiple untested branches in `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`
findings.detail:
Several branches of the new helper function have no direct test coverage:
1. `content_type=None` early-return (line 26) — only exercised implicitly.
2. `not content_type.startswith(b'multipart/form-data')` early-return (line 26-27) — no test passes e.g. `b'application/json'`.
3. No semicolon in bare `multipart/form-data` header (line 30) — `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` always includes a `;`.
4. Quoted boundary with surrounding whitespace or edge cases like `boundary=""` (line 33).
5. The `httpx.Request(...)` constructor path in `_models.py` (lines 336-347) is only tested through `Client.post`, not directly.

Adding direct unit tests for these branches would catch regressions in the parsing logic independently of the higher-level integration path.

findings.title: `encode_multipart_data` `boundary` parameter now lacks a default — may break existing callers
findings.detail: The `boundary` parameter was previously `Optional[bytes] = None` and now has no default value. Any direct caller of `encode_multipart_data(data, files)` that omits the `boundary` argument will now get a `TypeError` at runtime. While the diff shows `encode_request` always passes the argument, any external or test code calling this function directly (or any future refactor) will silently break. Restore the `= None` default or verify that no public/documented API exposes this function.

findings.title: Inline comment lacks capitalisation and punctuation
findings.detail: The comment `# parse boundary according to` starts with a lowercase letter and is incomplete. Consistent with the surrounding codebase style, it should be capitalised and complete, e.g. `# Parse boundary according to RFC 2046 §5.1.1.` with the URL on the same or a following line.

findings.title: Magic literal `b"boundary="` repeated — extract to a named constant
findings.detail: The byte string `b"boundary="` appears twice (once in the `startswith` guard and once in the slice). Extracting it to a module-level or local constant (e.g. `_BOUNDARY_PREFIX = b"boundary="`) eliminates duplication and makes future changes safer, consistent with the existing use of `CHUNK_SIZE` in the file.

findings.title: Parametrize decorator key style inconsistent with surrounding tests
findings.detail: The existing tests wrap the parametrize key in a tuple (e.g. `("key")`), while the new tests use a bare string `"header"`. Either form is valid pytest, but for consistency with the file's own style the new tests should match.

findings.title: `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` does not assert body validity
findings.detail: The test checks status code and the Content-Type header but does not verify that httpx generated a valid boundary and that the multipart body is well-formed (e.g. by parsing it with `cgi.parse_multipart` as the existing tests do). It is unclear whether this omission is intentional; a comment or a body assertion would clarify intent for future readers.

findings.title: Trailing whitespace not stripped from boundary value
findings.detail: When the boundary token has trailing whitespace before a semicolon (e.g. `boundary=+++ ;`), the code strips leading/trailing whitespace from the section via `.strip()`, but only strips double-quote characters via `.strip('"')`. It does NOT strip whitespace from the final boundary value. For example, given `boundary=+++ ;`, the section after splitting on `;` would be ` boundary=+++ `, and after `.strip()` becomes `boundary=+++`, so slicing off `boundary=` gives `+++ ` (with trailing space). The `.strip(b'"')` call only removes quote characters, not spaces. The boundary used will therefore be `b'+++ '` instead of `b'+++'`, which will not match the actual multipart delimiter encoded in the body. Fix: also call `.strip()` (whitespace) after stripping quotes, e.g. `section.strip()[len(b"boundary="):].strip(b'"').strip()`.

findings.title: Boundary parsing only considers sections containing `boundary=` after splitting on `;`, but the first section (`multipart/form-data`) is also checked
findings.detail: The split on `;` includes the first token `multipart/form-data` (or `multipart/form-data` with possible leading spaces). The `startswith(b"boundary=")` check after `strip().lower()` will never match that first section, so it's harmless—but the more subtle issue is that if the content-type does NOT contain `;`, the function returns `None` without ever checking for a boundary that might appear inline. However, per RFC 2046 the boundary always follows a `;`, so this is correct. No actual bug here, just noting for completeness.

findings.title: Using `self.headers.encoding` to encode the content-type header value may produce wrong bytes
findings.detail: `self.headers.encoding` defaults to `'ascii'` when headers are ASCII-compatible, but can fall back to `'latin-1'` or other encodings for non-ASCII header bytes. The `Content-Type` header value (e.g. `multipart/form-data; boundary=abc`) is always ASCII, but the encoding used to decode it from the internal raw bytes representation and then re-encode it back could introduce subtle mismatches if the headers object has been initialized with non-ASCII bytes in other headers, causing the encoding to be something other than ASCII. More critically, `self.headers.get("content-type")` returns a `str` decoded with `self.headers.encoding`, and then `.encode(self.headers.encoding)` re-encodes it — if the encoding changed between the two calls (unlikely but possible in theory), or if the encoding is not ASCII-compatible for the boundary characters, the resulting bytes could differ. A safer approach would be to use `.encode("latin-1")` or `.encode("ascii")` which are the correct encodings for HTTP header values per RFC 7230.

findings.title: `encode_multipart_data` signature changed to make `boundary` non-optional, potentially breaking callers
findings.detail: The `boundary` parameter was previously `Optional[bytes] = None` and is now `Optional[bytes]` (no default). Any call site that invokes `encode_multipart_data(data, files)` without passing `boundary` will now fail with a `TypeError`. While the diff shows `encode_request` always passes it, any other direct callers (e.g. in tests or user code) would break. Verify all call sites pass the argument.

findings.title: User-controlled multipart boundary injected without validation
findings.detail:
The `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` function extracts a boundary value directly from a user-supplied `Content-Type` header and returns it verbatim (after only stripping quotes). This boundary value is then used when constructing the multipart stream body.

RFC 2046 §5.1.1 restricts the boundary token to a limited set of characters (letters, digits, and a small set of punctuation). No validation of the extracted boundary against the RFC-defined character set is performed here. An attacker who can influence request headers (e.g. via an SSRF scenario, or in a library whose callers pass through untrusted input) could supply a boundary containing:
- CRLF sequences (`\r\n`), enabling HTTP header injection if the boundary leaks into generated headers
- Characters such as `--` which could break multipart framing and allow body injection / boundary confusion attacks
- Very long strings that could cause DoS

The library should validate the extracted boundary against the RFC 2046 allowed character set before accepting it, and raise a clear error (or fall back to a random boundary) when the value is invalid.

findings.title: Boundary extraction strips only double-quotes, not trailing whitespace and other edge cases — CRLF injection risk
findings.detail: The boundary is extracted with `section.strip()[len(b'boundary='):].strip(b'"')`. Only double-quote characters are stripped from the value. Any embedded `\r`, `\n`, or `\r\n` in the boundary value (which can appear in a user-supplied `Content-Type` header) will be passed straight through into the multipart stream preamble/delimiter lines and potentially into the `Content-Type` response header set by `get_headers()`. This constitutes a CRLF / header-injection risk. At a minimum, the code should reject or sanitize any boundary containing control characters.

findings.title: User-supplied Content-Type header bypasses library-generated boundary — trust boundary concern
findings.detail: When a caller explicitly sets a `Content-Type` header containing a `boundary` parameter, that boundary is now extracted and used verbatim to construct the multipart body. Previously the library always generated a safe, random boundary. Accepting an externally-supplied boundary widens the attack surface: any caller (or upstream code) that lets untrusted input flow into the `Content-Type` header can now influence the structure of the serialized multipart body. This should be documented clearly and the extracted value should be strictly validated before use.

findings.title: Inline comment lacks capitalisation and punctuation
findings.detail: The comment `# parse boundary according to` starts lower-case and has no trailing period. Every other standalone comment in the existing codebase starts with a capital letter (e.g. `# File-like interfaces should use 'read' directly.`, `# Otherwise iterate.`, `# This large tuple based API …`). Consider: `# Parse boundary according to RFC 2046 §5.1.1.` and move the URL to be part of that sentence or a second comment line.

findings.title: Magic literal `b"boundary="` repeated instead of using a named constant
findings.detail: The byte string `b"boundary="` appears twice on line 32-33 (once in the `startswith` guard and once in the slice `[len(b"boundary="):]`). Extracting it to a local variable (e.g. `_BOUNDARY_PREFIX = b"boundary="`) would avoid the duplication and make future changes safer. This pattern is consistent with how the existing file already names things like `CHUNK_SIZE`.

findings.title: Parametrize decorator style is inconsistent with surrounding tests
findings.detail: The pre-existing tests at lines 19 and 113 wrap the parametrize key in a tuple: `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("key"), …)` / `@pytest.mark.parametrize(("value"), …)`. The new `test_multipart_explicit_boundary` and `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` tests use just `"header"` (a bare string). While either is valid pytest, for consistency with the file's own style the new tests should use `("header")` (or the surrounding tests should all be updated together).

findings.title: `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` does not assert the generated boundary is present
findings.detail: The test only checks the status code and that the Content-Type header equals the input header, but does not verify that httpx generated *some* boundary and that the body is valid multipart. The existing `test_multipart` and `test_multipart_file_tuple` tests always parse the body with `cgi.parse_multipart` and assert the file content. Omitting that check leaves a readability gap: a reader cannot easily tell whether the absence of a body assertion is intentional or an oversight.

findings.title: No test for `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` with `content_type=None`
findings.detail: The function returns `None` early when `content_type` is `None`. While the integration path through `_models.py` passes `None` when no content-type header exists, there is no direct/unit test calling `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type(None)`. This path is exercised only implicitly through end-to-end tests that don't pass a content-type header at all, which makes the gap less critical but still worth noting.

findings.title: No test for non-multipart content-type passed to `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type`
findings.detail: The early-return branch `not content_type.startswith(b'multipart/form-data')` (line 26) is never directly exercised by any test. `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` passes a `multipart/form-data` header without a boundary, so the startswith check always passes there. A test with e.g. `content_type=b'application/json'` is missing.

findings.title: Quoted boundary with surrounding whitespace not covered by a direct unit test of the helper
findings.detail: The strip of quotes (`strip(b'"')`) is exercised indirectly via `test_multipart_explicit_boundary`, but the helper function `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` itself is never called directly in the tests. An edge case like a boundary with embedded spaces or a boundary value that is only quotes (e.g. `boundary=""`) has no coverage.

findings.title: No test for explicit boundary passed via `httpx.Request` constructor directly (not via `Client.post`)
findings.detail: The new boundary-extraction logic lives in `Request.__init__`. All new tests exercise it through `Client.post(headers={...})`. There is no test that constructs `httpx.Request('POST', url, files=..., headers={'content-type': 'multipart/form-data; boundary=...'})` directly, leaving the constructor path only implicitly covered. Any regression in how the constructor wires `content_type`/`encoding` would go undetected.

findings.title: No test for `multipart/form-data` with no semicolon at all (boundary-less, semicolon-less)
findings.detail: The branch `if b';' in content_type` is False when the header is exactly `b'multipart/form-data'` with no parameters. `test_multipart_header_without_boundary` uses headers that include `; charset=utf-8` (always has a semicolon), so the `else` branch that simply returns `None` for a bare `multipart/form-data` header is untested.

findings.detail:
The `get_multipart_boundary_from_content_type` function extracts a boundary value directly from a caller-supplied `Content-Type` header and returns it verbatim (after only stripping double-quote characters).
